coredata.dc:title: Biomass-based carbon dot-modified cerium oxide nanoparticles (BCDs@CeO2) efficiently promote Myriophyllum aquaticum to remove NH4 +-N and TP in eutrophic water

coredata.dc:description: Aquatic plants are widely used for eutrophication remediation. However, strong abiotic plant stress often limits their remediation efficiency. This study proposed biomass-based carbon dot-modified cerium oxide nanoparticles (BCDs@CeO2) with good biocompatibility to mitigate abiotic plant stress. The BCDs@CeO2 pretreated Myriophyllum aquaticum exhibited enhanced removal rates of NH4 +-N and TP, with the 10 mg/L BCDs@CeO2 treatment showing increases of 39.23 % and 29.11 %, respectively, compared to the pure water-precultured plant system (p < 0.05). Plant physiological changes and transcriptomic analysis revealed that BCDs@CeO2 treatment upregulated glycolysis/gluconeogenesis and α-linolenic acid metabolism pathways in rhizomes, inducing increased ATP synthase and antioxidant enzyme (Peroxidase, Catalase, and Superoxide Dismutase) activities, and enhancing amino acid metabolism, which further boosted Glutamine Synthetase activity and promoted NH4 +-N and TP absorption and utilization. ICP-MS and microscopic analysis confirmed the uptake and migration of BCDs@CeO2 in plants. In summary, this study provides an effective strategy to enhance eutrophication phytoremediation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the materials. (a) Fluorescence spectra of BCDs and BCDs@CeO2 at the optimum excitation wavelength (λex=320 nm). BCDs (1) and BCDs@CeO2 (2) under natural light and 365 nm UV light are shown in the top right corner. (b) TEM pictures (50 nm) of BCDs@CeO2. (c) FTIR spectra.

Changes of TN (a), NH4 +-N (b), NO3 --N (c), and TP (d) concentrations in eutrophic water in 10 and 20 mg/L BCDs, CeO2, and BCDs@CeO2 solution pretreated phytoremediation systems. Blank stands for plant-free system, and W is for pure water pretreated plant system.
First-order kinetic model for TN (a), NH4 +-N (b), NO3 --N (c), and TP (d) by different materials pretreated plant system.

Effects of treatments on plant growth (a), biomass accumulation (b), and chlorophyll content (c) of M. aquaticum after a 17-day N and P removal experiment. Different letters above each bar represent the significance of the difference at p < 0.05.

Effects of treatments on MDA content (a), POD activity (b), CAT activity (c), SOD activity (d), ATP synthase activity (e), and GS activity (f) of M. aquaticum after 17-day N and P removal experiment. Different letters above each bar represent the significance of the difference at p < 0.05.
Principal component analysis (PCA) after excluding several variables with less contribution.

Transcriptome data analysis. (a-b) Volcano plot of differentially expressed genes (DEGs). The vertical axis shows the significance level, while the horizontal axis indicates changes in gene expression. Gray dots represent non-differentially regulated genes, blue dots indicate down-regulated genes and red dots indicate up-regulated genes. (c-d), KEGG-enriched scatter plots for Ce-C10 vs. W and Ce-C20 vs. W. The vertical axis lists KEGG pathways, and the horizontal axis shows the enrichment factor. Larger dots mean more enriched genes, while redder dots indicate higher significance.

Content of Ce and mineral elements in plant tissues after BCDs, CeO2, and BCDs@CeO2 treatments. a, Distribution of Ce elements. b, Mineral elements in leaves. c, Mineral elements in rhizomes. Different lowercase letters on the bar graphs indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) between the means of various treatments.
Laser scanning microscope (LSM) images of cross sections from roots, stems, and leaves cultured with BCDs@CeO2 (20 mg/L) for 24 hours. All images were collected under the same exposure conditions.
(a) –(c) TEM images (1 μm) of M. aquaticum tissues cultured with 20 mg/L BCDs@CeO2 for 7 days. (d) –(f) TEM images (1 μm) of M. aquaticum tissues cultured with pure water for 7 days.
Mean values of parameters for the first-order kinetic model of nutrient uptake at different plant systems. Kν, nutrient removal rate constant; R2, goodness of fit.

head.ce:title: Biomass-based carbon dot-modified cerium oxide nanoparticles (BCDs@CeO2) efficiently promote Myriophyllum aquaticum to remove NH4 +-N and TP in eutrophic water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aquatic plants are widely used for eutrophication remediation. However, strong abiotic plant stress often limits their remediation efficiency. This study proposed biomass-based carbon dot-modified cerium oxide nanoparticles (BCDs@CeO2) with good biocompatibility to mitigate abiotic plant stress. The BCDs@CeO2 pretreated Myriophyllum aquaticum exhibited enhanced removal rates of NH4 +-N and TP, with the 10 mg/L BCDs@CeO2 treatment showing increases of 39.23 % and 29.11 %, respectively, compared to the pure water-precultured plant system (p < 0.05). Plant physiological changes and transcriptomic analysis revealed that BCDs@CeO2 treatment upregulated glycolysis/gluconeogenesis and α-linolenic acid metabolism pathways in rhizomes, inducing increased ATP synthase and antioxidant enzyme (Peroxidase, Catalase, and Superoxide Dismutase) activities, and enhancing amino acid metabolism, which further boosted Glutamine Synthetase activity and promoted NH4 +-N and TP absorption and utilization. ICP-MS and microscopic analysis confirmed the uptake and migration of BCDs@CeO2 in plants. In summary, this study provides an effective strategy to enhance eutrophication phytoremediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Eutrophication is a global water pollution problem triggered by excessive nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) emissions. According to the United Nations Sixth Earth Health Check report, N and P fluxes in the water environment have exceeded sustainable levels, posing a significant challenge to freshwater ecosystems and marine environments (Lian et al., 2022; Vuuren et al., 2019). Eutrophication-induced cyanobacterial outbreaks disrupt water ecology functions, leading to fish death and threatening human health by releasing algal toxins; they also affect the water purification process in water plants (He et al., 2016; Lakshmikandan et al., 2024). To mitigate the ecological and health problems caused by eutrophication, researchers develop physicochemical techniques, including dredging, in-situ chemical immobilization, and bioelectrochemical systems (Wang and Jiang, 2016; Zhang et al., 2014). However, these techniques are expensive, non-selective, and environmentally unsafe, while phytoremediation by aquatic plants is economical, efficient, and safe (Li et al., 2015).

Aquatic plants remove soluble N and P through inter-root uptake and rhizosphere microbial degradation from water ecosystems (Shelef et al., 2013). M. aquaticum, as a submerged plant, is widely used to mitigate nutrient loads and heavy metal pollution due to its rapid growth and adaptability (Cui et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2018). However, when used to treat high-concentration wastewater, total phosphorus (TP) and ammonia nitrogen (NH4 +-N) removal efficiencies of M. aquaticum are relatively low (Luo et al., 2017, 2018). Moreover, high concentrations of NH4 +-N affect the plant’s ion exchange and physiological properties, usually manifested as a decrease in Glutamine Synthetase (GS) activity related to the conversion of NH4 +-N (Gao et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2017). At NH4 +-N concentrations above 30 mg/L, the antioxidant response in M. aquaticum begins to become imbalanced, resulting in increased malondialdehyde (MDA) levels (Li et al., 2022). This oxidative imbalance usually accompanies decreased nutrient uptake (Huang et al., 2013). Therefore, enhancing the antioxidant capacity of aquatic plants may be an effective way to enhance the effectiveness of phytoremediation for eutrophication.

Nanomaterials have been proposed as a potential solution because they can help plants improve their antioxidant responses to various stresses (Usman et al., 2020). Studies have shown that metal oxide nanoparticles (e.g., Ag NPs, CuO NPs, ZnO NPs, CeO2 NPs, TiO2 NPs, and Fe3O4 NPs) can improve plant tolerance to drought, salinity, and heavy metal stresses (El-Saadony et al., 2022). Taking CeO2 NPs as an example, with the generation and elimination of oxygen vacancies in the crystal, cerium ions can be transformed between + 3 and + 4 valence to form a redox cycle (Deshpande et al., 2005); these properties have made it widely studied in agriculture and environmental protection. Several studies have shown that CeO2 NPs promote crop biomass and grain yield (Ma et al., 2015; Rico et al., 2014), protect plants from abiotic stresses (Rossi et al., 2017) and enhance plant resistance to microbial diseases(Alotaibi, 2023). Nanomaterials protect plants from environmental stresses by scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Wu et al., 2017); however, the positive effects depend on nanomaterials' surface physicochemical properties and dosage. Generally, only low concentrations or coating-modified nanomaterials positively impact plant growth (Jahani et al., 2019; Khan et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2017).

Based on the potential to enhance plant stress resistance, researchers have tried to combine nanomaterials with phytoremediation, hoping to reduce the phytoremediation cycle and improve remediation efficiency. In this process, contaminants can attach to nanomaterials or co-enter plants with them, migrating in plant tissues, resulting in more efficient pollution removal (Hou et al., 2023; Song et al., 2019). However, traditional nanomaterials are gradually replaced by carbon-based modified nanomaterials to avoid plant toxicity. For example, 100–1000 mg/kg starch-stabilized nano-ZVI iron can activate the antioxidant system of ramie while increasing the Cd accumulation in plants (Gong et al., 2017), activated carbon-loaded iron and silver nanoparticles can accelerate the removal of chlorfenapyr from wastewater through adsorption and plant uptake (Romeh and Saber, 2020). Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging carbon nanomaterials with fluorescent properties that have been found to promote photosynthesis (Swift et al., 2021). Their excellent hydrophilicity and biocompatibility are closely related to the carboxy, ester, and amino groups on CDs’ surfaces. (Cao et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2021, 2020). CDs limit heavy metal migration by promoting biomass accumulation, enhancing ion uptake, and reducing oxidative damage, thereby improving the efficiency of heavy metal phytoremediation (Cao et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2021). In addition, the thermal conversion of biowaste to generate low-toxicity biomass-based CDs (BCDs) gradually alternates chemical or electrolytic methods (Agnol et al., 2021). However, there is still a gap in the application of BCDs in phytoremediation, and the potential of BCDs-modified metal nanomaterials in this field has not yet been explored.

Therefore, this study prepared BCDs-modified cerium oxide (BCDs@CeO2) to explore their effects on eutrophication phytoremediation. Unlike previous studies (Chen et al., 2021; Hou et al., 2023), plants were pre-cultured with BCDs@CeO2 suspensions and then transferred to eutrophic water to study phytoremediation effects rather than co-remediation effects. To investigate the impacts of BCDs@CeO2 on phytoremediation, nutrient concentration changes, and plant physiological indicators were determined. Transcriptome analysis and the translocation analysis of BCDs@CeO2 in plant tissues were used to explain its potential mechanisms in enhancing eutrophication phytoremediation.

Commercial CeO2 and rice straw were used to synthesize biomass-based CDs-coated CeO2 suspension (BCDs@CeO2) by hydrothermal reaction. The commercial CeO2 was dispersed into distilled water by ultrasonication to form CeO2 dispersion (1000 mg/L). Then, 160 mL of dispersion was mixed with 0.16 g of rice straw powder; the mixed solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene liner and heated at 180 ℃ for 6 hours. After cooling to 25 ℃, the obtained brown solution was filtrated through four layers of gauze and was sonicated for 60 minutes to collect the supernatant. Then, the solution was dialyzed for deionization (MW=1000). Finally, the purified BCDs@CeO2 were freeze-dried for subsequent use. The BCDs were prepared from rice straw powder by a similar method and obtained through a 0.45 μm membrane. The details of the characterization processes are listed in the Supplementary Materials.

About 15 cm long, pest-free, well-grown aquatic plants (M. aquaticum) from a field pond were washed with tap water and deionized water to remove surface-adsorbed substances. M. aquaticum is widely used in constructed wetlands for N and P removal (Li et al., 2021). The plants were domesticated for 7 days in an artificial climate chamber, where temperature was 25°C, humidity was 86 %, photon flux density was 150 mmol m−2 s−1, and light duration was 10 hours/day.

The BCDs, CeO2, and BCDs@CeO2 suspension were prepared using an ultrasonic apparatus (UC-22.5H; Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd) with a half-strength Hogland nutrient solution (Zhang et al., 2021). When M. aquaticum was treated with 0 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 30 mg/L, 40 mg/L, and 50 mg/L of BCDs@CeO2 suspension for 7 days, it was observed that 30–50 mg/L of BCDs@CeO2 caused severe plant water loss (Fig. S1). Therefore, the final pre-cultivated BCDs@CeO2 suspension concentration was set to 10 mg/L and 20 mg/L; the same diluted BCDs and CeO2 suspension were compared. All plants were grown in 500 mL tall beakers with three replicates for each treatment. After cultivation, the plants were transferred to an artificial simulated eutrophic water, which was formed by adding 40 mg/L NO3 - (KNO3), 40 mg/L PO4 3- (KH2PO4), and 80 mg/L NH4 + ((NH4)2SO4) to the N-deficient and P-deficient Hoagland nutrient solution. The corresponding concentrations of NO3 --N, NH4 +-N, and TP were 9 mg/L, 63 mg/L and 13.3 mg/L, respectively. 25 mg/L chloramphenicol was added to inhibit the growth of microorganisms. In the N/P removal experiment, seven groups were established: CD10, CD20, Ce10, Ce20, Ce-C10, and Ce-C20 represent plant systems pretreated with 10 mg/L and 20 mg/L BCDs, CeO2, and BCDs@CeO2, respectively, W represents the pure water-precultured plant system. In addition, Blank represents the eutrophic water without plants. At any plant culture stage, the beakers were wrapped with aluminum foil to protect rhizomes from light. The initial fresh weight of each plant was recorded before cultivating and treating eutrophic water. Consistent with previous studies where the growth cycle of M. aquaticum in the experiments was only 17–19 days (Zhou et al., 2017), plants survived for 17 days in eutrophic water. The water losses via transpiration were replenished by deionized water every four days and then the whole solution was stirred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The uptake of N and P
ce:section.ce:para:
During the N and P removal experiment, 0.5 mL, 1 mL, 4 mL, and 1 mL water were withdrawn from each beaker on 0, 5, 9, 13, and 17 d to determine total nitrogen (TN), total phosphorus (TP), ammonium nitrogen (NH4 +-N), and nitrate nitrogen (NO3 --N) concentrations, respectively (MAPADA UV6100S Dual Beam UV-Vis Spectrophotometer). For each sample, 6.5 mL of deionized water was added again to mix. The TN, NH4 +-N, NO3 --N, and TP concentrations were determined by alkaline potassium persulfate digestion spectrophotometry, Nessler’s reagent spectrophotometry, UV spectrophotometry, and ammonium molybdate spectrophotometry, respectively, following the guidelines established by the Chinese Ministry of Environmental Protection (The State Environmental Protection, 2002) . The volumetric nutrient removal rate in the eutrophic solution was estimated using a modified first-order kinetic model, which is commonly used to assess nutrient removal rates in artificial wetlands (Li et al., 2021):

The relative biomass accumulation rate, chlorophyll content, malondialdehyde and H2O2 levels, as well as the activities of antioxidant enzymes (peroxidase, catalase, and superoxide dismutase), and enzymes associated with the N and P metabolism, specifically glutamine synthetase and ATP synthase, were determined by the methods listed in Supplementary Materials.

RNA from plant rhizomes was extracted using ethanol precipitation combined with the CTAB-PBIOZOL. The RNA extracts were then dissolved in 50 μL of DEPC-treated water. RNA quantity and quality were assessed with a Qubit fluorometer and Qsep400 high-throughput biofragment analyzer. To isolate mRNA, which typically contains a polyA tail in eukaryotes, mRNA was purified and fragmented into small pieces using fragmentation buffer at an optimal temperature. This was followed by reverse transcription into cDNA using random hexamer primers. Sequencing adapters were ligated, and DNA magnetic bead purification along with fragment selection were carried out to generate a library with insert sizes ranging from 250 to 350 bp. The cDNA was then amplified by PCR, purified, and re-assessed for quality and quantity. After the library passed quality control, the different libraries were pooled based on effective concentration and target sequencing output, and sequencing was performed to produce 150 bp paired-end reads. Sequencing was carried out by synthesizing and sequencing simultaneously. Fluorescently labeled dNTPs, DNA polymerase, and junction primers were introduced to the sequencing flow cell for amplification. During complementary strand synthesis for each sequencing cluster, the fluorescently labeled dNTPs emit specific fluorescence signals, which were captured by the sequencer and converted into sequencing peaks using software, yielding the sequence data for each fragment. DIAMOND software was employed for transcript annotation across seven major databases, and RSEM software was used to quantify transcript expression levels, with FPKM values calculated based on transcript length. Differential expression analysis between groups was performed using DESeq2, and KEGG enrichment analysis was conducted based on hypergeometric testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and transportation of BCDs@CeO2 in plants
ce:section.ce:para: The biological accumulation of Ce in all plants was determined using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 7700x, 7900) according to the method described by Hou et al. (2023). Concurrently, the concentrations of mineral elements (K, Ca, and Mg) in plant tissues were measured to examine the relationship between the presence of BCDs@CeO2 and plants' uptake of mineral elements. The concentrations of Ce, K, Ca, and Mg in the plant tissues were calculated based on dry weight. The ICP-MS assay procedure is provided in the Supplementary Materials.

Referring to previous studies, the adsorption of BCDs@CeO2 (20 mg/L) by M. aquaticum was tested with a confocal laser scanning microscope equipped with a 555 nm laser excitation (FV1200) (Li et al., 2016). To distinguish the fluorescence effects of BCDs@CeO2 from those presented by plant pigments, the plants were cultured with a suspension of rhodamine B-labeled BCDs@CeO2, and fluorescence was detected by slicing the plants' roots, stems, and leaves. The specific method for rhodamine B labeling BCDs@CeO2 is described in the Supplementary Materials. Additionally, the presence and distribution of BCDs@CeO2 in plants' root, stem, and leaf structures were characterized using transmission electron microscopy.

The changes in plant physiological indexes and nutrient concentrations in water were expressed as the three replications' mean and standard error (SE). Data were organized using Excel 2019 and analyzed with Origin 2021 (OriginLab, Northampton, MA, USA) to create graphs. Differences between treatments were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Significant differences (p < 0.05) were indicated by different letters according to Fisher LSD's multiple comparison test.

The biomass-based carbon dots-modified cerium oxide (BCDs@CeO2) dispersion appeared pale brown under natural light and emitted blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light. And the fluorescence intensity of BCDs@CeO2 under 365 nm UV light was lower than that of BCDs ( Fig. 1a). Consistent with the findings of (Gong and Dong, 2021), who observed the maximum fluorescence intensity of CDs at 439 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks of BCDs and BCDs@CeO2 were located at 438 nm and 441 nm, respectively, under optimal excitation at 320 nm. In contrast, the overall fluorescence intensity of BCDs@CeO2 in this study was lower than that of BCDs. According to Li et al. (2011), the fluorescence quenching in BCDs@CeO2 can be attributed to charge transfer and energy exchange between BCDs and CeO2.

Subsequently, the morphology of BCDs@CeO2 was analyzed. Compared to CeO2 with a medium particle size (15–30 nm) and clustered distribution (Fig. S2a), and BCDs with a smaller particle size (2.5–3.5 nm) and uniform distribution (Fig. S2b), BCDs@CeO2 has a slightly larger particle size (20–35 nm), indicating better dispersion than CeO2 (Fig. 1b). The TEM image of BCDs@CeO2 revealed that some CeO2 particles were coated with BCDs. Previous studies have confirmed that coating CeO2 with organic material reduces cellular damage from its sharp edges and also helps alleviate stress caused by clustering (Barrios et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2015).

FTIR analysis further revealed the chemical properties of BCDs@CeO2 (Fig. 1c). Strong absorption peaks at 3430 cm−1 for BCDs, CeO2, and BCDs@CeO2 were attributed to free water or O-H groups. The absorption peak at 1632 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CC or CO, and the broad absorption peak at 1082 cm−1 corresponded to C-O stretching vibrations in alcohols, ethers, or carboxyl groups. The weak absorption peaks at 784 cm−1 and 955 cm−1 were possibly due to C-H bending vibrations on the benzene ring. The similar absorption peaks of BCDs and BCDs@CeO2 indicated that the BCDs, rich in polar functional groups, were successfully attached to CeO2 (Deng et al., 2024). These polar functional groups endow BCDs@CeO2 strong hydrophilicity and biocompatibility, which promotes favorable interactions with polar molecules in plant cell walls. As a result, the nanomaterials can enter plant cells via apoplastic or symplastic pathways, modulating the plant’s nutrient absorption and stress response mechanisms (Dong et al., 2024; Li et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of BCDs@CeO2 on plant uptake of N and P
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the changes in N and P concentrations across different phytoremediation systems. Compared to the plant-free system (Blank), N and P concentrations significantly decreased in all plant systems, indicating the significant advantage of M. aquaticum in nutrient removal (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019). During the first 13 days, TN and NH4 +-N concentrations decreased continuously in most plant systems. On days 13–17, TN concentrations tend to be stable, while NH4 +-N concentrations increased (Fig. 2a-b). The increase of NH4 +-N concentrations at the final stage may be attributed to the secretion of low-molecular-weight (LMW) compounds by plants under a high NH4 +-N/NO3 --N ratio, which may have caused ammonia accumulation (Neumann and Römheld, 2007). The NO3 --N concentrations in all plant systems rapidly decreased by about 50 % in the first 5 days and then stabilized with small fluctuations (Fig. 2c). This indicates that, at the beginning of the experiment, plants primarily absorbed NO3 --N, while at the later stages, they preferred to absorb NH4 +-N. This phenomenon is consistent with the findings of Li et al. (2015), which emphasized the selective uptake of different N forms by plants in eutrophic water. Regarding TP removal, TP concentrations in all plant systems steadily decreased from 13.5 mg/L to below 6 mg/L, showing sustained P uptake (Fig. 2d)

Throughout the treatment process, only the Ce-C10 group consistently exhibited lower TN concentrations than the W group, and the NH₄⁺-N and TP concentrations in the Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 groups remained lower than those in the W group (Fig. 2a-b, d). According to Table S1, the NH₄⁺-N removal efficiencies in the BCDs (50.89–52.94 %) and CeO₂ (51.24–52.01 %) treatment groups were both higher than the W group (46.6 %), but no significant differences were observed. Similarly, the TP removal efficiencies in the BCDs (64.50–65.88 %) and CeO₂ (61.30–64.73 %) groups were higher than in the W group (57.6 %), without significant differences. In contrast, the NH₄⁺-N and TP removal efficiencies in the BCDs@CeO₂ treatment group were significantly increased by 31.03–39.23 % and 23.11–29.11 %, respectively (p < 0.05). Notably, the Ce-C10 group exhibited the highest removal efficiencies for NH₄⁺-N (64.88 %) and TP (74.37 %). In previous studies, Wang et al. (2020) indicated that CeO₂ NPs enhance N uptake in rice under low N levels but reduce N uptake at high N levels, thereby alleviating N stress. However, under normal N supply conditions, CeO₂ NPs exacerbate oxidative stress in rice. Abbas et al. (2020) reported that 500 mg/kg CeO2 NPs can promote P uptake by wheat (Triticum aestivum L.), but when the CeO2 NPs concentrations increased to 2000 mg/kg, P content in the roots and shoots decreased by 10 % and 19 %, respectively. Moreover, biochar was found to effectively mitigate the negative impacts of CeO2 NPs on plants, helping maintain normal N and P uptake even under a 2000 mg/L exposure concentration (Abbas et al., 2020). These findings suggest that CeO₂ regulates nutrient uptake in plants, and the modification of bio-based materials may enhance this regulatory capacity.

Fig. 3 and Table 1 show that the first-order kinetic models for TN, NH4 +-N, and TP removal in all plant systems exhibited good fit (R² > 0.85), while the first-order kinetic model for NO₃⁻-N removal showed a poorer fit (R² < 0.82). The nutrient removal rates were higher in the Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 groups. Specifically, the TN removal rate constant for Ce-C10 was the highest at 0.29499, while the NH₄⁺-N removal rate constants for Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 were 0.31112 and 0.27992, respectively. The TP removal rate constants, with excellent model fit (R² > 0.99), were 0.35148 and 0.32339, respectively. Additionally, it was observed that in the plant systems pretreated with materials, the NO₃⁻-N removal rate constants were generally lower than the W group, while the first-order removal rate constants for NH₄⁺-N were higher than the W group (Fig. 3b-c; Table 1). This result is consistent with the findings of Gao et al. (2016), suggesting that the faster plants uptake NH4 +-N, the slower they uptake NO3 --N. This observation corresponds to the changes in N and P concentrations (Fig. 2), further supporting the finding that BCDs@CeO2 enhances nutrient uptake in plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of BCDs@CeO2 on physiological characteristics of plants
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a shows that after 17 days of nutrient removal, no significant differences in plant growth were observed among all treatment groups, except that plants in the Ce10 and Ce20 groups were slightly shorter. Data analysis indicated no significant difference in biomass accumulation after 7 days of pretreatment; however, at the end of the nutrient removal period, BCDs treatment significantly promoted the increase in biomass accumulation and photosynthetic pigments (Fig. 4b-c) (p < 0.05). This result is consistent with the study by Hu et al. (2022), which demonstrated that CDs accelerate the rate of electron transfer, enhancing chlorophyll content, Rubisco activity, and photosystem II activity, thereby promoting plant growth. According to Muhie (2022), the increase in plant photosynthetic efficiency under abiotic stress is not always allocated to accumulate biomass but may be redirected to metabolic or other physiological processes. This explanation accounts for the phenomenon in the present study: compared to the W group, although the total chlorophyll in the Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 groups increased by 42.2–45.6 %, only a slight increase was shown for biomass accumulation.

Fig. 5a shows that the malondialdehyde (MDA) content in rhizomes is higher than in leaves, compared to the W group, the rhizomes MDA content in the Ce20 group increased significantly by 35.1 %, while decreased significantly by 26.9 % in the Ce-C10 group (p < 0.05). Li et al. (2022) reported that, in M. aquaticum cultured with eutrophic water and sediments, the MDA content in the leaves was higher than in the rhizomes. Xie et al. (2007) suggested that excessively grown algal in eutrophic water depletes oxygen, causing the rhizomes to produce stronger oxidative stress than the leaves. Therefore, experimental conditions may be a significant factor contributing to the differences in MDA content between the rhizomes and leaves. The strong oxidative stress response in the Ce20 group’s rhizomes confirms the negative impact of high concentrations of CeO2 on plants (Abbas et al., 2020). In contrast, the decreased MDA content in the Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 groups’ rhizomes suggests that the modification of biomass materials helps mitigate CeO2 toxicity. Barrios et al. (2016) reported that citric acid-modified CeO2 NPs can effectively enhance the photosynthetic pigments in plant leaves, and Wu et al. (2017) found that poly (acrylic acid) nanoceria (PNC) can effectively alleviate ROS levels in chloroplasts. Unlike MDA, the H2O2 content in plant tissues is not significantly different (Fig. S3), indicating the possible involvement of other ROS (e.g., ·OH, O2 -·) and that ROS scavenging efficiency varied among groups.

In plants, peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), and superoxide dismutase (SOD) are key components of the antioxidant defense system, playing a crucial role in reducing cellular damage by lowering ROS levels (Davies et al., 2009; Huang et al., 2013; Smirnoff and Arnaud, 2019). Fig. 5b-d shows that, compared to the W group, the Ce-C20 group exhibited the highest POD activity in rhizomes (299.6 % increase, p < 0.05). CAT activity in the leaves of Ce10, Ce20, Ce-C10, and Ce-C20 was significantly increased (p < 0.05), and in the rhizomes, CAT activity in Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 increased by 98.04 % and 111.21 %, respectively. Ce-C20 also had the highest SOD activity in rhizomes, with a 43.6 % increase (p < 0.05), followed by Ce-C10 with a 24.8 % increase. Several studies have shown that a certain amount of CeO2 NPs can act as free radical scavengers, helping plants mitigate oxidative stress (Jahani et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2015). However, when plants are exposed to high concentrations of CeO2 NPs, the transformation of Ce4+ to Ce3+ induces excessive ROS production, exceeding the regulation capacity of antioxidant enzymes, preventing the plants from maintaining antioxidant balance (Jahani et al., 2019). Additionally, studies have reported that, under the same concentration, coated CeO2 NPs can simultaneously enhance antioxidant enzyme activity and reduce oxidative stress levels (Barrios et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2017). These findings explain the results of this study: CeO2 treatment enhanced some antioxidant enzyme activities but did not reduce MDA levels, whereas BCDs@CeO2 treatment enhanced the activities of three antioxidant enzymes and reduced MDA levels.

ATP is a key signaling molecule in regulating plant P metabolism, and changes in ATP synthase activity directly affect P uptake efficiency (Ye et al., 2024). Fig. 5e shows that, compared to the W group, the Ce-C10 exhibited a significant 96.2 % increase in ATP synthase activity in rhizomes (p < 0.05), and the Ce-C20 group showed a 64.3 % increase. Ye et al. (2024) pointed out that, under high P level, plants maintain intracellular P metabolic balance by downregulating the expression of ATP synthase-related genes, preventing excessive P absorption. Therefore, the BCDs@CeO2 treatment likely promoted P removal by enhancing ATP synthase activity. Glutamine synthetase (GS) is a key enzyme in NH4 + assimilation, using ATP to synthesize glutamine from glutamate (Glu) and NH 4
+, thus supporting normal plant growth (Britto and Kronzucker, 2002; Thomsen et al., 2014). Fig. 5f shows that GS activity in the rhizomes of Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 groups increased by 76.67 % and 79.64 %, respectively, while Ce10 and Ce20 groups showed a decline. Wang et al. (2020) demonstrated that CeO2 NPs reduce GS activity, thus decreasing NH4 +-N absorption in rice (Oryza sativa), while Zhu et al. (2021) noted that decreased GS activity indicates NH4 +-N assimilation capacity decreased, and enhanced GS activity can alleviate NH4 +-N toxicity. However, in this study, although GS activity in rhizomes of CeO2-treated plants decreased, the NH4 +-N removal efficiency increased by 10.0–11.6 % compared to the W group (Table S1). Maybe CeO2 influences other NH4 +-N metabolic pathways, such as enhancing glutamate synthase (GOGAT) or glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) activity, to promote NH4 +-N assimilation (Liu et al., 2016). The enhanced GS activity in rhizomes of BCDs@CeO2-treated plants may be related to the altered physicochemical properties of CeO2 after BCDs modification. According to Meng et al. (2022), CDs-modified CeO2 possesses abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups and excellent ROS scavenging ability.

In Fig. S4, the first-order removal rate constants for TN, NH4 +-N, NO3 --N, and TP are represented by C_TN, C_NH

PCA results revealed that GS_rhizome, ATP synthase_rhizome, SOD_rhizome, CAT_rhizome, and POD_rhizome contributed most to PC1 and were positively correlated with it, while MDA_rhizome was negatively correlated ( Fig. 6, Table S5). Thus, it was concluded that BCDs@CeO2 pretreatment enhances NH4 +-N and TP removal efficiency by upregulating GS, ATP synthetase, SOD, CAT, and POD activities in rhizomes, thereby reducing MDA levels in rhizomes.

To investigate the molecular mechanisms of BCDs@CeO2's effect on nutrient absorption in M. aquaticum, transcriptomic analysis was conducted on rhizomes of the treated plants. The analysis involved three sample groups (W, Ce-C10, and Ce-C20, with 3 biological replicates for each), and differentially expressed genes (DEGs, |log2(Fold Change|≥1, and FDR <0.5) were identified to assess the effect of BCDs@CeO2 treatment on gene expression. Fig. 7a shows that, in the Ce-C10 group, 301 genes were differentially expressed, with 198 upregulated and 103 downregulated. Fig. 7b shows that in the Ce-C20 group, 1432 genes were differentially expressed, with 890 upregulated and 542 downregulated. KEGG analysis revealed that BCDs@CeO2 treatment significantly affected the transcriptome of M. aquaticum rhizomes, with DEGs in both Ce-C10 vs W and Ce-C20 vs W groups enriched in photosynthesis, photosynthetic antenna proteins, and metabolic pathways (Fig. 7c-d).

The specific KEGG pathway revealed that, compared to the W group, the Ce-C10 treatment significantly upregulated genes related to glycolysis/gluconeogenesis, such as those similar to phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK) and fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (FBPase) (4.1.1.49, 3.1.3.11 in Fig. S5), as well as genes involved in pyruvate metabolism (4.1.1.1 in Fig. S5). In the Ce-C20 treatment, genes similar to fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (FBPase) (2.7.1.90 in Fig. S7) were also upregulated. Glycolysis/gluconeogenesis plays a crucial role in plant responses to abiotic stress, energy supply, and enhancing antioxidant defenses (Bolouri-Moghaddam et al., 2010; Keunen et al., 2013). In this pathway, pyruvate is converted to acetyl-CoA and enters the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, the products of which further drive ATP synthesis, a process that relies on ATP synthase (Araújo et al., 2012). Therefore, BCDs@CeO2 pretreatment may enhance glycolysis/gluconeogenesis in plant rhizomes, thereby inducing an increase in ATP synthase activity to meet the energy demands of plants under high nutrient stress and enhancing P absorption and utilization (Fromm and Hargrove, 2012). In the α-linoleic acid metabolic pathway, the Ce-C10 treatment activated acetyl coenzyme A oxidase (ACX) and its downstream genes (Fig. S6), while Ce-C20 treatment activated the hydrolyzed peroxidative cleavage lyase 1 (HPL1) gene (Fig. S8). According to Chen et al. (2019) and Raza et al. (2020), activating these genes further promotes the synthesis of jasmonic acid, which can enhance plant stress resistance by reprogramming metabolic pathways.

Additionally, the DEGs heatmap showed that in the rhizomes of BCDs@CeO2-treated M. aquaticum, genes associated with glutathione, histidine, tryptophan, and tyrosine metabolism were upregulated (Fig. S9). Numerous studies have indicated that the enhancing amino acid metabolism helps neutralize ROS, coordinates phytohormone signaling with N metabolism, and maintains cellular homeostasis, thereby improving plant tolerance to salt stress, heavy metal stress, and nutrient imbalance (Corpas et al., 2021; Ji et al., 2022; Munir et al., 2019; Noctor et al., 2012). In the Ce-C10 vs. W group molecular function GO analysis, DEGs were significantly enriched in enzyme inhibitor, peptidase regulator/inhibitor, endopeptidase regulator/inhibitor, and serine-type endopeptidase inhibitor activities (Fig. S10). These regulators and inhibitors promote plant survival under stress by modulating key enzyme activities in metabolic pathways, reducing protein degradation and free amino acid secretion in response to salt, drought, and pathogens (Hellinger and Gruber, 2019; Santamaría et al., 2014). BCDs@CeO2 treatment significantly up-regulated the expression of these genes (Table S6), suggesting their association with the enhanced amino acid metabolism, which aids nutrient absorption and normal growth of M. aquaticum in eutrophic water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distribution of Ce and other mineral elements in plants
ce:section.ce:para: ICP-MS analysis was conducted to determine the Ce content and the concentrations of mineral elements (K, Ca, Mg) in M. aquaticum. The results revealed that the Ce content in the rhizomes of the Ce20, Ce10, Ce-C20, and Ce-C10 groups was significantly higher than in the leaves, with Ce content decreasing sequentially across the four groups ( Fig. 8a), indicating that the rhizomes were the primary uptake site and only a small amount of nanoparticles migrated to the leaves. Additionally, Ce accumulation significantly affected the distribution of mineral elements. Specifically, compared to the W group, K content increased, while Ca and Mg content decreased in the Ce10 and Ce20 groups (p < 0.05) (Fig. 8b, c, Table S7). Previous studies have demonstrated that CeO2 NPs can significantly impact crop nutrient uptake. For example, Rico et al. (2015) found that CeO2 NPs promoted the absorption of K and Mg, but excessive K absorption inhibited wheat spike development. Another study by Rossi et al. (2016) reported increased Mg uptake and elevated chlorophyll levels due to CeO2 treatment. Furthermore, Rico et al. (2014) showed that, within reasonable limits, CeO2 NPs did not significantly affect the levels of K, Ca, or other mineral nutrients in plant tissues. In the rhizomes of W, CD10, and CD20 groups, higher Ca and lower K content was observed. Hepler (2005) suggested that severe Ca deficiency inhibits root development and apical growth, while excess Ca inhibits K uptake, thereby affecting enzyme activation and metabolic processes. The imbalance of K and Ca uptake in CeO2 and BCDs-treated plants likely explains the lower activities of antioxidant enzymes and nutrient metabolism-related enzymes (CAT, ATP synthase and GS). In contrast, the Ce-C10 and Ce-C20 groups exhibited more balanced levels of K, Ca, and Mg, demonstrating a more stable distribution of mineral elements.

The migration and distribution of BCDs@CeO2 in plant tissues were visually examined using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). CLSM images, processed with ZEISS ZEN 3.8 software, revealed that rhodamine B-labeled BCDs@CeO2 emitted red fluorescence under 555 nm excitation. Fluorescence signals were detected in roots, stems, and leaves, with the strongest signal in the roots. In stems, fluorescence was concentrated in the vascular tissues, while in leaves, the fluorescence indicated the migration of BCDs@CeO2 or free BCDs to the leaves ( Fig. 9). No fluorescence signals were observed in the tissues of M. aquaticum cultured in pure water (Fig. S11). The high specific surface area of root hairs accelerates nanoparticle uptake, which may explain the strongest fluorescence signal in the roots (Gong and Dong, 2021). Li et al. (2016) reported that when young plant roots are immersed in a CDs suspension, the particles easily migrate from root vascular tissues to stem tissues. Additionally, the root system of hydroponic M. aquaticum grows around the stem, and the developed aeration tissues further promote nanoparticle entry. TEM images showed that BCDs@CeO2 were irregularly distributed in the root cell walls and intracellularly, forming distinct nanoclusters. These nanoparticles entered the stem through aeration pores and aggregated outside the stem cells ( Fig. 10a, b). In contrast, no such aggregation was observed in the roots or stems of the pure water-treated M. aquaticum (Fig. 10d-e). Although no nanoparticle aggregation was observed in the TEM images of leaves (Fig. 10c, f), the presence of bulges around the cell walls (Fig. 10c) indicated that BCDs@CeO2 treatment altered the physiological structure of the leaves. Marchiol et al. (2016) found that CeO2 NPs easily entered the thin-walled tissues of leaves, distributing in the chloroplast stroma and vacuoles. Yang et al. (2017) observed CeO2 nanoclusters in watercress chloroplasts after high-concentration CeO2 treatment, which significantly reduced chloroplast size. Chloroplast disruption typically affects plant chlorophyll, but studies have also shown that Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) coated CeO2 is more effective in promoting photosynthesis than uncoated CeO2 (Cao et al., 2017). These findings suggest that BCDs@CeO2 accumulate in the roots of M. aquaticum, with a small amount migrating through the stem to the leaves, where they impact the physiological structure and promote the synthesis of photosynthetic pigments.

The present study showed that BCDs@CeO2 prepared by hydrothermal reaction had good water solubility and biocompatibility. They could enter M. aquaticum and migrate into plants' roots, stems, and leaves. After pretreatment with BCDs@CeO2, M. aquaticum exhibited more balanced mineral element uptake and significantly improved removal efficiency of NH4 +-N and TP. The biomass accumulation, chlorophyll content, antioxidant enzyme activities (POD, SOD, CAT), and N/P conversion-related enzyme activities (ATP synthase and GS) in 10 mg/L BCDs@CeO2 pretreated plants increased after 17 days. Transcriptomic analyses revealed that BCDs@CeO2 upregulated glycolysis/gluconeogenesis pathways in rhizomes, inducing enhanced ATP synthase activity, which enabled M. aquaticum to more efficiently uptake P in eutrophic water. In addition, BCDs@CeO2 enhanced amino acid metabolism by up-regulating related enzyme modulators or inhibitors. They activated the antioxidant system in response to oxidative stress in a high N-P environment through up-regulation of α-linolenic acid metabolism and enhanced uptake of NH4 +-N and TP. These results contribute to a deeper understanding of N and P removal mechanism of aquatic plants enhanced by biomass-based nanomaterial pretreatment and provide a new, green, and viable control strategy for phytoremediation of eutrophic water.

Nie Xingling: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ma Jun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Li B.Larry: Writing – review & editing. Chen Ming: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.
